questions.question: Explain why the 'HttpOnly' flag is an important defense mechanism against session hijacking. (2-3 marks)
expectedPoints: It prevents client-side scripts from accessing the cookie
This directly mitigates the risk of session token theft via Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities
It is a simple server-side setting that adds a significant layer of protection

questions.question: Compare and contrast Session Hijacking through packet sniffing and Session Fixation. Describe how each attack works and recommend a primary defense for each. (5-10 marks)
expectedPoints: Definition of Session Sniffing: Passive interception of network traffic to steal a session token after user login
Primary Defense for Sniffing: Mandatory use of HTTPS (encryption) and the Secure cookie flag
Definition of Session Fixation: Attacker sets the session token before login and gains access after the victim authenticates
Primary Defense for Fixation: Regenerating the session ID immediately after successful login
Key Contrast: Sniffing steals a token, Fixation provides a token; one is post-authentication, the other is pre-authentication
Additional defenses: Using long/random tokens, implementing session timeouts
